skill.name: biogeobears
skill.description: Set up phylogenetic biogeographic analyses with BioGeoBEARS in R. The skill validates tree and geography inputs, prepares analysis folders, customizes an RMarkdown workflow, and explains model outputs.
skill.summary: Prepare, validate, and document BioGeoBEARS ancestral range analyses.
skill.author: brunoasm

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows most are false positives from markdown code fences, BioGeoBEARS model names, formulas, and relative path examples. The confirmed risks are legitimate local command execution, dependency installation from GitHub, and local file writes used by the analysis workflow. No prompt injection attempt, credential access, hidden network exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Local Command Execution Workflow
medium_findings.description: The static external command findings are mostly markdown examples and RMarkdown chunks, not Ruby execution. The skill still instructs users to run Python, bash, and R commands on local research files, so commands should be reviewed before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and intended for local workflow setup. No malicious intent is visible, but execution uses user-provided paths and generated scripts.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled File Writes
medium_findings.description: The Python helper writes reformatted data to the output path supplied on the command line. The RMarkdown workflow also creates output directories and writes result files, which is legitimate but can overwrite files if paths are chosen poorly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-write calls are direct and confirmed in executable helper files. The risk is bounded to local filesystem impact and appears workflow-related.

medium_findings.title: External Package Installation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes instructions to install BioGeoBEARS from a GitHub repository and links to external project resources. This is expected for the analysis domain, but it introduces normal dependency supply-chain risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is documented as package installation and references, not hidden exfiltration. Community dependency installation still requires user review.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: The high static weak-cryptography alerts match scientific terms such as DEC, AIC, and model notation. No cryptographic API, hashing routine, or encryption workflow was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged terms are part of BioGeoBEARS model names and statistical formulas. There is no evidence of cryptographic code in these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive Relative Path Examples
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts refer to relative paths used in generated analysis examples, such as input and results directories. They are not shown as a traversal exploit, although path choice should still be kept inside the project workspace.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative paths are visible in documentation and RMarkdown parameters. They support expected folder layout rather than accessing sensitive parent directories.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL alerts are citations and project resource links. No evidence found that the skill silently sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in citation and further-resource sections. The reviewed code does not perform HTTP requests to these locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Command Execution Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The static external command findings are mostly markdown examples and RMarkdown chunks, not Ruby execution. The skill still instructs users to run Python, bash, and R commands on local research files, so commands should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and intended for local workflow setup. No malicious intent is visible, but execution uses user-provided paths and generated scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python helper writes reformatted data to the output path supplied on the command line. The RMarkdown workflow also creates output directories and writes result files, which is legitimate but can overwrite files if paths are chosen poorly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file-write calls are direct and confirmed in executable helper files. The risk is bounded to local filesystem impact and appears workflow-related.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Package Installation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes instructions to install BioGeoBEARS from a GitHub repository and links to external project resources. This is expected for the analysis domain, but it introduces normal dependency supply-chain risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is documented as package installation and references, not hidden exfiltration. Community dependency installation still requires user review.

content.value_statement: Phylogenetic biogeography projects often fail because tree, range, and model files are hard to prepare consistently. This skill validates inputs, builds a BioGeoBEARS RMarkdown workflow, and explains the resulting model comparison outputs.
actual_capabilities: Validates BioGeoBEARS geography files for tab delimiters, species names, and binary area codes.
Reformats CSV or TSV distribution tables into PHYLIP-like BioGeoBEARS input files.
Compares tree tip labels with geography file species names when a Newick tree is provided.
Creates an organized analysis folder structure with inputs, scripts, results, and documentation.
Customizes an RMarkdown template for DEC, DEC+J, DIVALIKE, DIVALIKE+J, BAYAREALIKE, and BAYAREALIKE+J models.
Generates guidance for interpreting AIC, likelihood ratio tests, parameter estimates, and ancestral range plots.
limitations: Requires local R, Python 3, BioGeoBEARS, and supporting R packages to run the analysis.
Does not prove that a tree is biologically appropriate for the research question.
Large trees or many geographic areas can require long runtimes outside the skill setup step.
Generated results still require domain review before publication or scientific claims.

use_cases.title: Prepare an island biogeography analysis
use_cases.description: Set up validated inputs and an RMarkdown workflow for species distributed across islands or regions.

use_cases.title: Standardize comparative model testing
use_cases.description: Run a consistent DEC, DIVALIKE, BAYAREALIKE, and +J model comparison across related phylogenies.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot geography file errors
use_cases.description: Find formatting, delimiter, species name, and binary code problems before a BioGeoBEARS run.

prompt_templates.title: Validate my files
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate my BioGeoBEARS inputs. My tree file is [tree path] and my geography file is [geography path]. Tell me what must be fixed before running models.

prompt_templates.title: Create an analysis workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Build a standard project folder and RMarkdown run
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a BioGeoBEARS analysis for [tree path] and [distribution path]. Use [number] areas, maximum range size [number], and compare all six standard models.

prompt_templates.title: Customize model selection
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose models and parameters for a specific study design
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose BioGeoBEARS models for a phylogeny with [tip count] tips and [area count] areas. Explain the tradeoffs for maximum range size and +J models.

prompt_templates.title: Interpret completed results
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review after the RMarkdown report is generated
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my BioGeoBEARS model comparison and plots from [results path]. Summarize AIC support, parameter estimates, uncertainty, and caveats for a manuscript methods section.

output_examples.input: A tree file and a CSV distribution table with five named areas.
output: A validated geography file ready for BioGeoBEARS.
A project folder with input, scripts, results, and documentation directories.
A clear list of any species name mismatches to fix.

output_examples.input: A request to compare DEC, DIVALIKE, BAYAREALIKE, and +J models.
output: A customized RMarkdown workflow for the requested models.
Instructions for installing required R packages and running the analysis.
Expected output names for reports, result objects, and plots.

output_examples.input: Completed BioGeoBEARS results with model comparison tables.
output: A plain-language explanation of the best-supported model.
Notes on dispersal, extinction, and founder-event estimates.
Cautions about model uncertainty and biological interpretation.

best_practices: Keep all generated files inside a dedicated project directory for reproducibility and safer path handling.
Validate tree tip labels and geography species names before starting long model runs.
Report model uncertainty when AIC weights are similar across candidate models.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated shell or R commands without checking paths and output locations.
Do not use maximum range sizes that are biologically unrealistic only because they are computationally possible.
Do not treat the best AIC model as final evidence without checking parameter estimates and data quality.

faq.question: Does this skill run BioGeoBEARS by itself?
faq.answer: It prepares and documents the workflow. The actual model run requires local R, BioGeoBEARS, and user approval to execute commands.

faq.question: What input files are required?
faq.answer: You need a rooted Newick tree and a geography table with species names and area presence or absence values.

faq.question: Can it reformat my distribution spreadsheet?
faq.answer: Yes. The included Python helper can convert common CSV or TSV layouts into BioGeoBEARS geography format.

faq.question: Which models does the template support?
faq.answer: It supports DEC, DEC+J, DIVALIKE, DIVALIKE+J, BAYAREALIKE, and BAYAREALIKE+J model comparisons.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses local scripts, file writes, and package installation guidance. These are expected, but users should review commands first.

faq.question: Can the results be used directly in a paper?
faq.answer: The reports can support manuscripts, but all methods, parameters, and biological conclusions need expert review.
